steps.prompt: What does 'sacra' mean in this context?

hints.sacred: While 'sacer' often means 'sacred', it also means 'accursed/devoted to destruction' — here Vergil condemns the hunger for gold
hints.safe: 'safe' would be 'tutus' or 'salvus' — 'sacra' comes from 'sacer' meaning accursed here

hints.Genitive: Genitive feminine singular of 'sacer' would be 'sacrae' — 'sacra' is nominative feminine
hints.Accusative: Accusative feminine singular of 'sacer' would be 'sacram' — 'sacra' is nominative, agreeing with 'fames'
hints.Ablative: Ablative feminine singular of 'sacer' would be 'sacra' (same form), but here it is nominative modifying 'fames'

hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'fames' would be 'famis' — 'fames' is the nominative form
hints.Accusative: Accusative of 'fames' would be 'famem' — 'fames' is the nominative form
hints.Vocative: While this is exclamatory, 'fames' here is nominative — the exclamation addresses the concept

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'famis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nouns have genitive in -i — 'famis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension nouns have genitive in -ei — 'famis' ends in -is
